hook.text: Massive objects bend light. A galaxy cluster can warp and magnify the image of objects behind it—a cosmic magnifying glass.

buildup.text: Einstein predicted this. Arthur Eddington confirmed it in 1919 by measuring starlight bent around the Sun during an eclipse.

discovery.text: Strong lensing creates arcs and multiple images. JWST has captured stunning lensed images of the most distant galaxies known.

twist.text: Lensing reveals invisible mass. The way light bends tells us exactly where dark matter is concentrated in a cluster.

climax.text: Weak lensing—subtle distortions across millions of galaxies—maps dark matter on the largest scales in the universe.

punchline.text: Gravity bends light around massive objects. The cosmos has its own telescope.

quiz.question: How does gravitational lensing help scientists study dark matter?
options: The way light bends reveals where invisible dark matter is concentrated
Dark matter emits lensed light that telescopes can detect
Lensing destroys dark matter, making it briefly visible
